content.name: SoulBot Chat v1.0.0 — Memory Manager
content.summary: Three-tier memory management: working (session context), episodic (conversation logs), semantic (extracted knowledge). Handles consolidation with conflict detection, decay, proactive recall for companion behaviors, session summary generation, semantic similarity search, semantic relevance context assembly, satisfaction tracking, token-budget-aware context assembly, and GDPR-compliant data operations.
content.description: Memory management module for SoulBot Chat (Pattern E sub-module). Called by main.aisop.json for all memory I/O. Manages three tiers: working memory (current session context with sliding window), episodic memory (per-session conversation logs), and semantic memory (extracted durable knowledge — facts and preferences). Supports consolidation (episodic to semantic compression), intelligent decay (recency+access+base scoring), context assembly with token budgeting, recall search across tiers, and GDPR-compliant export/deletion. All I/O via file_system with atomic write safety. Token estimate: ~1000-3000 tokens per operation (context assembly + file_system I/O + semantic search). Circuit-breaker default: 3 failures in 60s window.

operation.agentic_prompt: Memory operation type: load_context, save_turn, recall, consolidate, decay, export, delete, proactive_recall, save_session_summary

session_id.agentic_prompt: Current conversation session identifier. Used to locate episodic/{session_id}.json.

user_id.agentic_prompt: User identifier. Used to locate profiles/{user_id}.json and scope data operations.

memory_dir.agentic_prompt: Root directory for all memory files. Contains working.json, episodic/, semantic/, profiles/.

turn_data.agentic_prompt: Conversation turn to save: {user_input, agent_response, timestamp, emotion_detected}

session_data.agentic_prompt: Session data for summary generation: {turns, topics_discussed, emotional_arc, key_moments}

current_topics.agentic_prompt: Current session topics for proactive recall relevance scoring.

token_context.agentic_prompt: Accept from main router. Contains { budget_remaining: number, utilization_percentage: number, budget_warning: boolean }. When budget_warning=true, LoadContext applies aggressive summarization.

content.system_prompt:
Memory operations manager. Ensure data integrity with atomic writes. Respect user privacy. Never expose raw memory data without user request. Mirror User's exact language and script variant.
Align: Human Sovereignty and Benefit.

aisop.main:
graph TD
    Start[Validate Inputs] --> OperationGate{Route by Operation}
    OperationGate -- load_context --> LoadContext[Assemble Working Memory + Semantic]
    OperationGate -- save_turn --> SaveTurn[Log to Episodic + Update Working]
    OperationGate -- recall --> RecallMemory[Search Episodic + Semantic]
    OperationGate -- consolidate --> ConsolidateMemory[Compress Episodic to Semantic]
    OperationGate -- decay --> DecayPrune[Score and Prune Old Memories]
    OperationGate -- export --> ExportData[GDPR Data Portability]
    OperationGate -- delete --> DeleteData[GDPR Right to Erasure]
    OperationGate -- proactive_recall --> ProactiveRecall[Proactive Follow-up Search]
    OperationGate -- save_session_summary --> SaveSessionSummary[Generate Session Summary]
    LoadContext --> ContextRelevanceScorer[Score Context Relevance]
    ContextRelevanceScorer --> endNode((End))
    SaveTurn --> endNode
    RecallMemory --> endNode
    ConsolidateMemory --> endNode
    DecayPrune --> endNode
    ExportData --> endNode
    DeleteData --> endNode
    ProactiveRecall --> endNode
    SaveSessionSummary --> endNode

Start.step1: Receive operation, session_id, user_id, memory_dir, query, and turn_data from main router. Resolve memory_dir to absolute path relative to AISOP working directory.
Start.step2: Validate required parameters by operation: load_context requires session_id and user_id. save_turn requires session_id, user_id, and turn_data. recall requires user_id and query. consolidate requires user_id. decay requires user_id. export requires user_id. delete requires user_id. proactive_recall requires user_id and session_id. save_session_summary requires session_id, user_id, and session_data. Missing required parameter -> inform caller with specific missing field name.
Start.constraints: (A) Type guard — operation must be one of: load_context, save_turn, recall, consolidate, decay, export, delete, proactive_recall, save_session_summary. All string parameters must be strings. turn_data must be object with required fields {user_input, agent_response, timestamp}. (B) Size guard — reject any string parameter >5000 chars. (C) Injection guard — strip shell metacharacters and control characters from all inputs. (D) Path guard — reject '..' in any path component, validate all resolved paths fall within memory_dir. session_id and user_id must match pattern [a-zA-Z0-9_-] only. (E) Encoding guard — NFC normalize all string inputs. (F) File lock guard — all write operations must acquire advisory lock ({target_file}.lock) before proceeding. Lock files are auto-cleaned if older than 60 seconds (stale lock recovery).
Start.Error: Validation fails -> (no retry: deterministic validation errors do not benefit from retry or circuit-breaker) -> fallback: inform caller with specific constraint violation and required corrections. Do not proceed to OperationGate.

OperationGate.step1: Route by validated operation: load_context -> LoadContext. save_turn -> SaveTurn. recall -> RecallMemory. consolidate -> ConsolidateMemory. decay -> DecayPrune. export -> ExportData. delete -> DeleteData. proactive_recall -> ProactiveRecall. save_session_summary -> SaveSessionSummary.
OperationGate.constraints: Exactly one route. Operation already validated in Start — unknown operation cannot reach this node.
OperationGate.Error: Unknown operation (unreachable via Start validation) -> fallback: default to load_context as safest operation -> inform caller.

LoadContext.step1: Read {memory_dir}/working.json via file_system. If not found OR file is malformed (invalid JSON or missing required fields), attempt .bak recovery: read {memory_dir}/working.json.bak via file_system. If .bak exists and is valid (valid JSON with turns array), use .bak contents as working memory, log WARNING 'Recovered from backup: working.json.bak used because working.json was missing or malformed'. If .bak is also missing or malformed, THEN initialize empty working memory: { schema_version: '1.0', session_id, user_id, turns: [], summary: '', started_at: now, last_updated: now }. Validate schema: must contain turns array.
LoadContext.step2: Read {memory_dir}/semantic/facts.json and {memory_dir}/semantic/preferences.json via file_system. If not found, initialize: { schema_version: '1.0', items: [] } and write to disk with atomic .tmp + rename. If found but missing schema_version, add schema_version='1.0' in-place (migration). Read {memory_dir}/profiles/{user_id}.json for user profile context. If not found, treat as empty object.
LoadContext.step3: Assemble context using SEMANTIC RELEVANCE RANKING with CONTEXT ROT PREVENTION: CONTEXT ROT PREVENTION: more context tokens does not always improve LLM performance. Prioritize context quality over volume. For each candidate context item, compute relevance_score = 0.5*topic_similarity + 0.3*recency + 0.2*emotional_weight. Only include items with relevance_score >= 0.3. Cap total context at budget limit even if more relevant items exist — diminishing returns above 3000 tokens. (a) Always include the most recent 3 turns verbatim (immediate conversational continuity — exempt from relevance_score threshold). (b) For remaining turn budget (up to 7 additional turns from working memory), rank by composite score: relevance_score = 0.5 * topic_similarity(turn.topics, current_session_topics) + 0.3 * recency_score(turn.timestamp) + 0.2 * emotional_weight(turn.emotion_detected). topic_similarity: count overlapping topic keywords between turn topics and working_context.last_topics, normalized 0-1. recency_score: linear decay from 1.0 (most recent) to 0.0 (oldest in working memory). emotional_weight: turns with non-neutral emotion_detected score 1.0, neutral score 0.5 (emotional turns are more contextually significant). APPLY THRESHOLD: discard candidate turns with relevance_score < 0.3 (context rot prevention — low-relevance items degrade LLM performance). Select top-7 from remaining candidates by relevance_score. Apply same relevance_score >= 0.3 threshold to semantic facts and preference items before inclusion. (c) For turns older than the selected window, generate compressed summary (key topics, decisions made, unresolved questions). TOKEN BUDGET MANAGEMENT: (1) NORMAL MODE (token_context is null OR token_context.budget_warning=false): estimate assembled context size — if total exceeds 3000 tokens (approx 12000 chars), progressively compress: first reduce older turn summaries, then trim semantic facts to top-K most relevant to current session topics, then truncate profile to essential fields only. Even in normal mode, cap total context at 3000 tokens — diminishing returns above this threshold degrade response quality. (2) AGGRESSIVE MODE (token_context.budget_warning=true): reduce working_turns window from 10 to 5, select only top-3 most relevant semantic facts, compress all turns beyond most recent 2 to single-line summaries, truncate profile to name + mood_baseline + top_interest only, cap total assembled context at 1500 tokens (approx 6000 chars). Log mode='aggressive' in context metadata for downstream awareness.
LoadContext.step4: Return assembled context object: { working_turns: [...last 10], history_summary: string, semantic_facts: [...relevant], user_preferences: [...], user_profile: {...}, token_estimate: number }.
LoadContext.constraints: Read-only operation — never modify files during context loading. Semantic facts ranked by relevance to recent conversation topics. Profile fields: name, relationship_notes, communication_style, key_topics.
LoadContext.Error: File read fails -> retry once -> circuit-breaker (3 failures in 60s) -> fallback: return minimal context with only available data and flag missing sources -> inform caller which tier(s) are unavailable.

ContextRelevanceScorer.step1: RECEIVE assembled context from LoadContext: working_turns, semantic_facts, user_preferences, history_summary. Also receive current user_message (passed through from main router via operation parameters).
ContextRelevanceScorer.step2: SCORE EACH CONTEXT ITEM for relevance to the current user message. For each working_turn: relevance = 0.4*semantic_similarity(turn.topics, current_message_topics) + 0.3*recency_bonus(turn.timestamp) + 0.2*emotional_continuity(turn.emotion, current_detected_emotion) + 0.1*entity_overlap(turn.entities, current_message_entities). For each semantic_fact: relevance = 0.5*topic_match(fact.content, current_message) + 0.3*fact_confidence + 0.2*access_frequency_bonus.
ContextRelevanceScorer.step3: SELECTIVE INJECTION: apply relevance threshold. Items with relevance >= 0.4 are INCLUDED in final context (high relevance). Items with 0.2 <= relevance < 0.4 are SUMMARIZED (compressed to one-line mention). Items with relevance < 0.2 are EXCLUDED (would add noise, degrade response quality). This prevents context rot — injecting irrelevant memories degrades LLM performance.
ContextRelevanceScorer.step4: BUDGET ENFORCEMENT: after relevance filtering, check total context token estimate. If exceeding budget (3000 tokens normal, 1500 aggressive), drop lowest-relevance items first until within budget. Always preserve: most recent 2 turns (conversational continuity), any crisis-flagged items, user-stated identity facts.
ContextRelevanceScorer.step5: Return enhanced context: { working_turns: [scored_and_filtered], semantic_facts: [scored_and_filtered], excluded_count: number, total_relevance_score: float, context_quality: 'high'|'medium'|'low', token_estimate: number }. context_quality = 'high' if avg relevance >= 0.6, 'medium' if >= 0.4, 'low' otherwise.
ContextRelevanceScorer.constraints: Scoring must be fast — simple keyword/entity overlap, not full semantic embedding (that would be too expensive per-turn). Never exclude safety-critical context (crisis flags, minor_mode indicators). Relevance scoring is transparent in metadata but not exposed to user.
ContextRelevanceScorer.Error: Scoring fails -> fallback: pass through LoadContext output unmodified (skip scoring) -> inform caller.

SaveTurn.step1: Validate turn_data structure: required fields {user_input: string, agent_response: string, timestamp: string (ISO 8601)}. Optional: {emotion_detected: string, topics: string[], intent: string, satisfaction_rating: float(0-1)|null}. Generate turn_id: timestamp + random 4-char hex suffix.
SaveTurn.step2: EPISODIC WRITE: OPTIMISTIC LOCKING: before writing, read current file's last_modified timestamp (or turn count). Compare against expected version from initial read. If mismatch detected (concurrent write by another session/process), re-read file, merge new turn into latest version, then write. This prevents lost updates from concurrent access. Read {memory_dir}/episodic/{session_id}.json via file_system. If not found, initialize: { schema_version: '1.0', session_id, user_id, created_at: now, turns: [] }. Append turn_data with turn_id to turns array. WRITE SAFETY: write to {memory_dir}/episodic/{session_id}.json.tmp first, then rename to {memory_dir}/episodic/{session_id}.json. Backup: copy existing file to {session_id}.json.bak before overwrite.
SaveTurn.step3: WORKING MEMORY UPDATE: OPTIMISTIC LOCK CHECK: compare working.json last_modified against expected. If stale, re-read and merge before writing. Read {memory_dir}/working.json. If schema_version field is missing, set schema_version='1.0' (in-place migration). Add turn to turns array. If turns array length > 10, compress oldest turns beyond 10 into summary field (extract key topics and decisions, append to existing summary). Update last_updated timestamp. WRITE SAFETY: write to working.json.tmp, rename to working.json, backup to working.json.bak.
SaveTurn.step4: Return confirmation: { saved: true, turn_id, episodic_file: session_id, working_turns_count: number, summary_updated: boolean }.
SaveTurn.constraints: Both writes (episodic and working) must succeed. If episodic write succeeds but working write fails, retry working write — do not rollback episodic. Atomic write pattern: .tmp then rename for every write. .bak backup for every overwrite. Never lose user conversation data. FILE LOCKING STRATEGY: all file writes use advisory file locking (create {filename}.lock before write, remove after). Lock acquisition timeout: 5 seconds. If lock cannot be acquired, retry once after 1s delay, then fail with 'concurrent_write_conflict' error. This complements optimistic locking with pessimistic prevention for high-contention scenarios.
SaveTurn.Error: Write fails -> retry once with explicit directory creation via file_system -> circuit-breaker (3 failures in 60s) -> fallback: return turn_data as JSON string for caller to cache in-memory, flag persistence failure -> inform caller to retry save_turn later.

RecallMemory.step1: Parse query into search terms. Extract key entities (names, dates, topics, events). Normalize: lowercase, remove stop words, expand abbreviations.
RecallMemory.step2: EPISODIC SEARCH: List files in {memory_dir}/episodic/ via file_system. For each session file, read and search turns for matches in user_input and agent_response fields. SEMANTIC MATCHING: beyond exact keyword matching, identify semantic equivalents — EN: 'mom' matches 'mother', 'happy' matches 'joy/excited'; ZH: '妈妈' matches '母亲', '开心' matches '高兴/快乐'; topic-level matches (asking about 'cooking'/'做菜' finds turns mentioning specific recipes). Score each match: (0.5 * semantic_relevance + 0.5 * term_frequency) * recency_bonus (newer sessions score higher). Collect top-10 candidate turns across all sessions.
RecallMemory.step3: SEMANTIC SEARCH: Read {memory_dir}/semantic/facts.json and {memory_dir}/semantic/preferences.json. Search for query term matches in fact content and preference descriptions. Score: exact match > partial match > related topic match.
RecallMemory.step4: MERGE AND RANK: Combine episodic and semantic results. Final score = 0.4 * relevance_score + 0.35 * recency_score + 0.25 * source_quality (semantic facts score higher quality than raw episodic). Return top-K results (K=5 default): { results: [{ source: 'episodic'|'semantic', content: string, session_id?: string, timestamp?: string, relevance_score: number }], total_searched: number, query_terms: [...] }.
RecallMemory.constraints: Read-only operation. Limit episodic file reads to most recent 50 sessions for performance. If >50 session files exist, prioritize by file modification date (newest first). Never return raw internal scoring data to end user — only formatted content with source attribution.
RecallMemory.Error: Search fails -> retry once -> circuit-breaker (3 failures in 60s) -> fallback: return empty results with message 'Memory search temporarily unavailable' -> inform caller.

ConsolidateMemory.step1: List files in {memory_dir}/episodic/ via file_system. Identify sessions older than consolidation threshold (default: 7 days since last turn timestamp). Skip sessions already marked as consolidated (check metadata field 'consolidated: true').
ConsolidateMemory.step2: For each qualifying session, read full conversation log. Extract durable facts: (a) User-stated biographical facts (name, age, occupation, location, family). (b) Stated opinions and preferences (likes, dislikes, communication style). (c) Recurring topics (mentioned in 2+ sessions). (d) Relationship milestones (first meeting, shared experiences, established rapport). (e) Explicit requests to remember something. Classify each extraction with confidence: HIGH (user explicitly stated) / MEDIUM (inferred from context) / LOW (single mention, tentative).
ConsolidateMemory.step2b: ENTITY RELATIONSHIP EXTRACTION (Mem0g-inspired, 68.4% accuracy improvement): From each qualifying session's conversation log, extract entity relationships as structured tuples: { subject: string, relation: string, object: string, confidence: float(0-1) }. Examples: {subject: 'user', relation: 'prefers', object: 'concise_answers', confidence: 0.85}, {subject: 'user', relation: 'works_at', object: 'company_name', confidence: 0.90}, {subject: 'user', relation: 'lives_in', object: 'city_name', confidence: 0.95}. Subjects and objects are normalized to lowercase identifiers. Relations use a controlled vocabulary: prefers, works_at, lives_in, knows, studies, likes, dislikes, owns, uses, related_to. Read {memory_dir}/semantic/{user_id}/relationships.json via file_system. If not found, initialize: { schema_version: '1.0', relationships: [] } and create {memory_dir}/semantic/{user_id}/ directory if not exists. MERGE STRATEGY: for each extracted relationship, check if an existing relationship with same subject+relation pair exists. If yes, update the object and confidence with the newer extraction (prefer_newer), preserve the old value in a history array: { old_object, replaced_at }. If no existing match, append as new relationship. WRITE SAFETY: atomic .tmp + rename, .bak backup. This creates a graph-like semantic memory structure enabling richer contextual retrieval.
ConsolidateMemory.step3: MERGE FACTS: Read {memory_dir}/semantic/facts.json. For each extracted fact: if it updates an existing fact (same topic/entity), CONFLICT DETECTION — if new fact contradicts existing (e.g., 'lives in NYC' vs stored 'lives in LA'), mark conflict: { old_value, new_value, conflict_detected_at, resolution: 'prefer_newer' }. Log conflict for transparency. Merge — keep higher confidence, update timestamp, preserve history including conflict record. If new fact, append. WRITE SAFETY: atomic .tmp + rename, .bak backup. Write updated facts.json.
ConsolidateMemory.step4: MERGE PREFERENCES: Read {memory_dir}/semantic/preferences.json. Same merge logic as facts. Categories: communication (formal/casual, emoji usage, response length), topics (favorite subjects, avoid topics), emotional (comfort style, humor appreciation), practical (scheduling, reminders). WRITE SAFETY: atomic .tmp + rename, .bak backup. Write updated preferences.json.
ConsolidateMemory.step4c: HIERARCHICAL SUMMARIZATION (progressive compression — research-backed: Recursive Summarization, Context Rot mitigation): Apply three-level compression based on session age: LEVEL 1 — RECENT (sessions < 7 days old): keep individual turn summaries at near-verbatim detail (full user_input and agent_response preserved, only strip filler words and redundant pleasantries). LEVEL 2 — OLDER (sessions 7-30 days old): compress to session-level summaries (one summary paragraph per session covering: main topics discussed, key decisions, emotional tone, unresolved items; individual turns are replaced by the session summary). LEVEL 3 — ANCIENT (sessions 30+ days old): compress to topic-level facts only (extract atomic fact statements, discard conversational context and narrative flow; only durable knowledge survives — e.g., 'user prefers morning meetings', 'user is allergic to shellfish'). For each processed session, apply the appropriate compression level based on the age of its last turn timestamp relative to current date. Write compressed versions back to episodic files with metadata { compression_level: 1|2|3, compressed_at: now }. WRITE SAFETY: atomic .tmp + rename, .bak backup.
ConsolidateMemory.step5: Mark consolidated sessions: update each processed episodic file with metadata { consolidated: true, consolidated_at: now, compression_level: 1|2|3 }. WRITE SAFETY: atomic write.
ConsolidateMemory.step6: Return summary: { sessions_processed: number, facts_extracted: number, facts_merged: number, preferences_extracted: number, preferences_merged: number, relationships_extracted: number, relationships_merged: number, compression_applied: { level1_count: number, level2_count: number, level3_count: number } }.
ConsolidateMemory.constraints: Never delete episodic data during consolidation — only mark as consolidated. LOW confidence extractions are stored but flagged for verification at next user interaction. Consolidation is idempotent — re-running on already-consolidated sessions is a no-op.
ConsolidateMemory.Error: Extraction fails for one session -> skip that session, continue others -> log skipped session_id. Write fails -> retry once -> circuit-breaker (3 failures in 60s) -> fallback: return partial results with list of failed sessions -> inform caller.

DecayPrune.step1: Read {memory_dir}/semantic/facts.json and {memory_dir}/semantic/preferences.json via file_system. For each memory item, calculate importance score: importance(t) = recency_weight * e^(-lambda * age_days) + access_weight * log(1 + access_count) + base_importance. Default weights: recency_weight=0.5, lambda=0.01, access_weight=0.3, base_importance=0.2.
DecayPrune.step2: Classify by score: (a) RETAIN (score >= 0.4) — no action, keep as-is. (b) DEMOTE (0.15 <= score < 0.4) — mark as 'low_priority', reduce detail level in context assembly. (c) ARCHIVE (score < 0.15) — compress to one-line summary, move to archived section within same file. Never auto-delete.
DecayPrune.step3: PROTECTED ITEMS: Never decay or archive: (a) User-stated identity facts (name, birthday, family). (b) Items with confidence HIGH. (c) Items explicitly requested to be remembered ('please remember...'). (d) Items accessed in last 30 days regardless of age. Protected items keep minimum score of 0.5.
DecayPrune.step4: Apply decay decisions. Update facts.json and preferences.json with new scores, statuses, and archived entries. WRITE SAFETY: atomic .tmp + rename, .bak backup for each file.
DecayPrune.step5: EPISODIC CLEANUP: For episodic sessions older than 90 days AND marked consolidated, compress full turns to summary-only (keep first turn, last turn, and summary — remove intermediate turns). WRITE SAFETY: atomic write. Never delete session files.
DecayPrune.step6: Return report: { total_items_scored: number, retained: number, demoted: number, archived: number, protected: number, episodic_compressed: number }.
DecayPrune.constraints: Never auto-delete any user data without explicit consent. Archive means compress and deprioritize, not remove. Decay runs are idempotent. Score calculation must be deterministic given same inputs. All writes atomic with backup.
DecayPrune.Error: Scoring fails -> retry once -> circuit-breaker (3 failures in 60s) -> fallback: skip decay cycle, return error report with reason -> inform caller. Partial write failure -> do not apply remaining changes, report which files succeeded and which failed.

ExportData.step1: Compile all user data across tiers. Read: (a) {memory_dir}/profiles/{user_id}.json — user profile. (b) {memory_dir}/episodic/*.json — all session logs where user_id matches. (c) {memory_dir}/semantic/facts.json — filter facts attributed to user_id. (d) {memory_dir}/semantic/preferences.json — filter preferences for user_id. (e) {memory_dir}/working.json — current session state if user_id matches.
ExportData.step2: Assemble portable export object: { export_version: '1.0.0', exported_at: ISO timestamp, user_id: string, profile: {...}, episodic_sessions: [{session_id, turns, created_at, last_turn_at}...], semantic_facts: [...], semantic_preferences: [...], working_memory: {...}|null, metadata: { total_sessions: number, total_turns: number, total_facts: number, date_range: {earliest, latest} } }.
ExportData.step3: Write export to {memory_dir}/exports/{user_id}_export_{timestamp}.json via file_system. Create exports/ directory if not exists. WRITE SAFETY: atomic .tmp + rename.
ExportData.step4: Return: { export_file: string (path), size_bytes: number (estimated), contents_summary: { sessions: number, turns: number, facts: number, preferences: number } }.
ExportData.constraints: Include ALL user data — nothing omitted. Export format must be self-contained and human-readable JSON. Do not include internal scoring metadata or system-only fields (decay scores, consolidation flags). Sanitize: remove any references to other users' data if present in shared sessions.
ExportData.Error: Read fails for one tier -> continue with available tiers, note missing sections in export metadata -> retry failed reads once -> circuit-breaker -> fallback: partial export with clear inventory of what is included and what is missing -> inform caller.

DeleteData.step1: CONFIRMATION GATE: This operation requires explicit confirmation from the caller. If no confirmation flag is set (confirmed: true in turn_data or explicit caller signal), return { requires_confirmation: true, message: 'Data deletion is irreversible. Confirm to proceed.', data_inventory: {sessions: N, facts: N, preferences: N, profile: exists/missing} }. Do NOT proceed without confirmation.
DeleteData.step2: PRE-DELETE BACKUP: Before any deletion, trigger ExportData operation internally to create a full backup export at {memory_dir}/exports/{user_id}_pre_delete_{timestamp}.json. WRITE SAFETY: atomic write. If backup fails, ABORT deletion — inform caller backup must succeed before deletion can proceed.
DeleteData.step3: DELETE PHASE — execute in order: (a) Delete {memory_dir}/episodic/{session_id}.json for all sessions matching user_id. Also delete corresponding .tmp and .bak files. (b) Remove user entries from {memory_dir}/semantic/facts.json and {memory_dir}/semantic/preferences.json — read, filter out user_id entries, write back. WRITE SAFETY: atomic. (c) Delete {memory_dir}/profiles/{user_id}.json and its .tmp/.bak files. (d) If {memory_dir}/working.json belongs to user_id, reset to empty state.
DeleteData.step4: AUDIT TRAIL: Read {memory_dir}/audit/deletion_log.json via file_system. If not found, initialize: { schema_version: '1.0', entries: [] }. Create audit/ directory if not exists. Append entry: { user_id, deleted_at: ISO timestamp, items_deleted: { sessions: number, facts: number, preferences: number, profile: boolean, working: boolean }, backup_file: string (export path) }. WRITE SAFETY: atomic .tmp + rename.
DeleteData.step5: Return: { deleted: true, user_id, items_deleted: {...counts}, backup_file: string, audit_logged: true }.
DeleteData.constraints: IRREVERSIBLE after completion — hence mandatory backup before delete. Confirmation is non-negotiable — never skip step 1. Audit log must survive deletion (it contains no user content, only metadata). If any deletion step fails mid-way, report partial deletion state — do not silently continue. Caller must be informed which items were deleted and which remain.
DeleteData.Error: Backup fails -> ABORT, do not delete anything -> inform caller. Partial deletion failure -> halt remaining deletions -> return partial report with what was deleted and what remains -> retry remaining items once -> circuit-breaker -> fallback: return detailed error state for manual intervention -> inform caller.

ProactiveRecall.step1: FOLLOW-UP SEARCH: Read {memory_dir}/semantic/facts.json and {memory_dir}/semantic/preferences.json. Identify items tagged with temporal markers (dates, deadlines, EN: 'next week', 'tomorrow', 'coming up'; ZH: '下周', '明天', '快到了', '马上要'; upcoming events). Check current date against event dates — collect items due within 7 days as upcoming_events: [{ event, date, reminder }].
ProactiveRecall.step2: UNRESOLVED TOPICS: Read {memory_dir}/working.json. Scan recent turns for questions asked but not fully answered, topics interrupted by digression, pending slot fills from previous sessions. Collect unresolved_topics: [{ topic, turns_since_mentioned }]. Limit to 3 most recent.
ProactiveRecall.step3: FOLLOW-UP OPPORTUNITIES: Read recent {memory_dir}/episodic/ sessions. Identify topics where user expressed ongoing interest or shared plans ('I am going to try that recipe', 'I have an interview next week'). Cross-reference with semantic facts for relevance. Collect follow_ups: [{ topic, last_mentioned, suggested_prompt }]. Limit to 2 most relevant.
ProactiveRecall.step4: Return proactive_context: { follow_ups, upcoming_events, unresolved_topics }. If no proactive items found, return empty arrays.
ProactiveRecall.constraints: Read-only operation. Proactive items must be genuinely relevant — do not fabricate connections. Limit total proactive items to 5 to avoid overwhelming the conversation. Temporal calculations use current date. Only suggest follow-ups for topics mentioned in last 14 days.
ProactiveRecall.Error: Search fails -> retry -> circuit-breaker -> fallback: return empty proactive_context -> inform caller.

SaveSessionSummary.step1: Receive session_data: { turns, topics_discussed, emotional_arc, key_moments }. Generate concise session summary: 2-3 sentences covering main topics, emotional trajectory, and notable interactions (corrections, milestones, new facts learned).
SaveSessionSummary.step2: Read {memory_dir}/episodic/{session_id}.json. Append session_summary field: { summary_text, topics: [], emotional_arc: string, key_facts_learned: [], duration_turns: number, generated_at: timestamp }. WRITE SAFETY: atomic .tmp + rename, .bak backup.
SaveSessionSummary.step3: Update {memory_dir}/working.json — set session_ended: true, session_summary: summary_text. This marks the session for consolidation eligibility.
SaveSessionSummary.step4: Return: { summary_generated: true, summary_text, session_id, topics_count, facts_learned_count }.
SaveSessionSummary.constraints: Summary must be factual — only include what actually happened in the session. Do not embellish or interpret. Keep summary under 500 characters. If session had fewer than 3 turns, generate minimal summary.
SaveSessionSummary.Error: Summary generation fails -> retry -> circuit-breaker -> fallback: save raw session metadata without narrative summary -> inform caller.
